input.title: Assessing Central Tendency in Admissions Data
input.setup_prose: As an admissions officer, you are reviewing the Quantitative Reasoning scores of 200 applicants to the Summer Scholars program. You need a summary value to represent the 'typical' performance of the pool, but you notice a few exceptionally high scores on the far right of the distribution.
worked_steps: Step 1: Inspect the distribution shape — notice that the histogram is not perfectly symmetrical, indicating the data is skewed.
Step 2: Locate the 'middle' — visualize where the majority of the data points cluster to estimate the center.
Step 3: Calculate the mean — sum all scores and divide by the count, noting how extreme high values pull this average away from the visual center.
input.interpretation: The mean is a classic example of central tendency. In this admissions scenario, it provides a 'center' point for the applicant pool's scores. However, because our distribution is slightly skewed by a few exceptionally high scores, the mean is pulled upward, potentially misrepresenting the 'typical' applicant. Understanding this helps admissions officers balance summary metrics with the actual range of data.

chart.caption: Distribution of Quantitative Reasoning scores (n=200) for Summer Scholars applicants.
chart.provenance: Simulated data based on institutional admissions benchmarks.

input.title: Interpreting Central Tendency in Skewed Data
input.setup_prose: As an administrator in the Office of Undergraduate Education, you are reviewing course evaluation scores for 102 seminars. Most students provided very high ratings, but a small handful of courses received notably lower marks.
input.prompt: Before answering, take a moment: what do you notice about this distribution? Based on the summary statistics and the histogram, which statement best describes how we should interpret the central tendency of these course evaluations?

options.text: The mean is the most accurate representation of the typical experience.
options.feedback: You might choose this if you look only at the arithmetic average (4.2), but the mean is pulled down by the small number of very low scores.

options.text: The median better represents the typical course rating in this distribution.
options.feedback: Because the distribution is skewed left by a few low scores, the median (4.5) better represents the 'typical' high-scoring course than the mean (4.2).

options.text: Mean and median are identical here, so either measure is acceptable.
options.feedback: This is a common reaction if you assume all distributions are perfectly symmetrical. However, the 'tail' to the left indicates this is not a normal distribution.

options.text: The mean is higher than the median because the distribution is skewed right.
options.feedback: You might select this if you equate the 'center' purely with the mathematical average, but this ignores how extreme low scores influence the mean.

input.hint: Observe the 'tail' of the histogram. Does the bulk of the data sit to the left or the right of the peak?

chart.caption: Distribution of course evaluation scores for introductory seminars in the Faculty of Arts and Sciences.
chart.provenance: Office of Undergraduate Education data audit 2023.

viz_decision.reason: rule-5: previous turn already used chart → vary or omit

input.title: Interpreting Central Tendency in Skewed Data
input.setup_prose: As an institutional researcher, you are reviewing course evaluation data to help department chairs identify courses needing support. You notice a cluster of low scores pulling the mean down, despite most students giving the course a high rating.
input.prompt: Looking at this data on student satisfaction scores for an undergraduate seminar, notice that the distribution is heavily skewed. How would you interpret the difference between the mean score of 3.2 and the median score of 4.5?
consider_scaffolds: What is your conclusion regarding the typical student experience for this course?
Which specific metric (mean or median) supports your conclusion best, and why?
What additional information about the distribution would help you confirm your interpretation?
input.rubric_primary_criterion: A strong response demonstrates an understanding that the median is more resistant to outliers than the mean in a skewed distribution.

dictionary_handoff.handoff_question: Is this how your school uses this term?

dictionary_handoff.handoffQuestion: Is this how your school uses this term?

viz_decision.reason: rule-3: constructive tier → learner constructs interpretation, no visual crutch

input.title: Interpreting Engagement Metrics
input.setup_prose: You are meeting with the departmental curriculum committee to review engagement data for a foundational course. You observe that while most students complete the weekly problem set in approximately three hours, a small group of students reports spending over fifteen hours. Consequently, the mean completion time appears much higher than the median.
input.prompt: When reviewing the summary metrics for total time spent by students on a specific core course assignment, you find that the mean is significantly higher than the median. How would you interpret this discrepancy to your department faculty, and what might it suggest about the students' experience?
input.rubric_primary_criterion: The learner demonstrates an ability to logically relate the influence of outliers or distribution shape (skew) to the discrepancy between central tendency metrics.

dictionary_handoff.handoff_question: These terms came up: mean, median, skewed. Which of these did your reasoning depend on, and how does your team use it?

dictionary_handoff.handoffQuestion: These terms came up: mean, median, skewed. Which of these did your reasoning depend on, and how does your team use it?

viz_decision.reason: rule-2: WikiDraft tier → contribution-routine flowchart (process, not content)

input.framing_prose: During annual course evaluation reviews, team leads often debate whether a single number adequately represents student satisfaction. You might see a focus on the median to avoid the influence of extreme outliers. Where does this tension between different measures of central tendency show up for you, or is there a case where a specific measure just doesn't capture the full picture? The course team reads every reflection to help shape future modules.
input.draft_template_hint: Consider how your team describes 'typical' results during busy seasons.
input.title: When this comes up in your work

flowchart.provenance: Internal module design standards for collaborative knowledge building.
